critique.summary: Regenerator 2000 is an interactive disassembler for the 6502 CPU, primarily targeting Commodore 8-bit computers. It features a text user interface (TUI) with modern conveniences such as cross-references, undo/redo, keyboard navigation, and integration with the VICE debugger.
mainPoints: Supports full 6502 instruction set including undocumented opcodes.
Provides side-by-side views of disassembly with hex dumps, sprites, bitmaps, charsets, and blocks.
Includes a debugger panel with live connection to VICE for breakpoints and stepping.
Supports multiple Commodore platforms like C64, C128, and Plus/4.
Can import various file formats including .prg, .crt, .d64, .d71, .d81, .t64, .vsf, .bin, .raw, and project files.
Exports assembly source compatible with assemblers such as 64tass, ACME, Kick Assembler, and ca65.
Offers project management features like saving/loading projects and auto-analysis of code/data regions.
Customizable display and navigation features including jump to address, cross-reference inspection, and history navigation.
Includes a Model Context Protocol (MCP) server for programmatic access.
Available for installation via Cargo and supports recommended modern terminals for best experience.
critique.truthfulness: The claims about Regenerator 2000's features and capabilities are consistent with the information available on its official GitHub repository and documentation. The project is actively maintained, supports a wide range of file formats and assemblers, and integrates with the VICE emulator debugger as stated. The technical details about supported platforms, file formats, and features align with known standards for 6502 disassembly tools and Commodore 8-bit systems. There is no indication of false or exaggerated claims in the article.
considerations: The article does not mention any potential limitations or known issues with the software.
There is no discussion of performance benchmarks or comparisons with other 6502 disassemblers.
User experience details such as learning curve or community support beyond Discord are not covered.
Security considerations when connecting to external tools like VICE or running the MCP server are not addressed.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers around tools for disassembling and analyzing 6502 code, with users sharing their own projects and experiences. There is appreciation for modern, accessible tools and recognition of the creators' backgrounds.

comments.author: s-macke
comments.text:
Nice to see an MCP integration here as well. In my experience, coding agents are great at analyzing MOS6502 code. Because the code is limited to only 64 kB, it does not overwhelm the agent. And in parallel it can write specs and even extract assets via normal coding tools.

Using my similar tool [0], I feel I get roughly a 100x speedup. I will definitely try regenerator2000.

[0] https://github.com/s-macke/OpcodeOracle
comments.analysis: This comment provides a thoughtful perspective on the utility of coding agents for analyzing 6502 code, supported by a relevant example and a linked project, making it highly accurate and constructive.

comments.author: robindeitch
comments.text:
Looks super cool...

I built a similar thing, primarily for my own fun. As a reaction to various C64 tools being scattered/old/unsupported exes and often with not OSX builds, my approach was to build a low-friction web app, which I could mess around with easily across whatever machine I was sitting in front of, whenever I had a few moments (kids...)

https://46c.io/

Examples : https://46c.io/project/NVXAZ7JY/code/ram/0E2B https://46c.io/project/8VM2EY7T/library

Basically : select a byte (or a range with shift) and use the buttons at the top to tag it with some metadata like a label, a comment, mark it as the lo/hi byte of a pointer etc - and it'll update the disassembly immediately. It saves all your work in browser local storage by default but if you sign in you can work from 'the cloud' (cheapo Firebase account) - I haven't shared widely before so no idea how that will hold up to the HN effect...

Enjoy!
comments.analysis: The comment shares a personal project with practical motivations and features, adding value to the discussion by offering an alternative solution and relevant links.

comments.author: ciroduran
comments.text: Ricardo Quesada is also one of the creators of cocos2d, he's been around for a while!
comments.analysis: This comment provides contextual background about the author of the tool, which is factual and adds credibility, though it is less substantive about the tool itself.

comments.author: kimi
comments.text:
Wish I had something like that when I was coding demos around 1988... :-)

(Edit): you kids have it easy.
comments.analysis: This nostalgic comment expresses a sentiment rather than technical insight, contributing little factual content but adding a human perspective.
